title: Pull-apart dinner rolls
subtitle: Soft enriched rolls baked touching in a tin
excerpt: Twelve soft, milk-enriched rolls pressed together in a rectangular tin and baked until they fuse at the sides. Pull them apart at the table to reveal pale, soft interior faces and a golden top. The butter-and-milk dough stays tender for two days.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the enriched dinner roll section describes milk-and-butter rolls arranged touching in a greased tin so the sides bond during proving. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: small enriched rolls placed in contact on a baking tin are documented as a method for producing soft-sided bread suited to the dinner table, where the rolls separate cleanly rather than tear.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cool completely and freeze in a bag. Refresh from frozen in a 180°C oven for 8 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Double to 24 rolls using two tins of the same size. No changes to the dough.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Shape the rolls, arrange in the tin, cover and refrigerate overnight. Bring to room temperature for 30 minutes before baking.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf. Rolls in a tin colour more slowly than individual rolls — the sides stay pale and soft

glossaryTerms.definition: When rolls are placed touching in a tin before their second rise, the gaps close as they prove and the sides bond during baking. The surfaces that were in contact remain soft and pale; only the exposed tops crust. This is what makes pull-apart rolls separate cleanly rather than tear.

content.text: Pull-apart rolls are baked in a tin rather than spaced on a tray. The rolls go in touching from the moment they start their second rise. The gaps close as they prove and the sides fuse during baking. At the table they separate cleanly, with a soft, pale face on each side and a golden top. The dough is enriched with butter and milk for a tender crumb that stays soft the next day.

Combine the {{strong-bread-flour}}, {{yeast-fast-action}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{caster-sugar}} in a large bowl, keeping the yeast and salt on opposite sides. Rub the {{unsalted-butter}} through the flour until no visible pieces remain. Mix the {{whole-milk}} and {{water}} and pour into the bowl. Work to a soft, slightly tacky dough, then knead for 8 minutes by hand until smooth and elastic.
Cover the bowl with a damp cloth and leave in a warm place for 60 minutes until doubled.

Butter the tin. Tip the dough onto a lightly floured surface and divide into 12 equal pieces (about 70 g each). Shape each into a tight ball: flatten the piece, fold the edges into the centre, turn seam-side down, then roll under a cupped hand in a firm circular motion until round and taut. Arrange in the tin in a 3×4 grid with each roll just touching its neighbours. This is the start of the
: the gaps will close as the rolls rise.
Cover loosely with oiled clingfilm and leave for 40 minutes until the rolls are puffed and the gaps between them have closed. Preheat the oven to 190°C fan.

Brush the tops carefully with the beaten {{eggs}} egg. Bake for 20–22 minutes until the tops are deep gold and the rolls feel set when pressed lightly in the centre. An instant-read thermometer inserted into a centre roll should read 93°C. Cool in the tin for 5 minutes, then turn out onto a rack. Serve warm.
